FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; Year 2 data (2018–2020); baseline ages 9–10, assessed at ages 10–13; structural MRI data from 7,614 participants.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD neuroimaging data; reported association between social media use and brain morphology using region-of-interest and vertexwise FEMA analysis.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in NeuroImage (2026); DOI: 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2026.121860.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (hours per day) and social media addiction (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) are independent variables paired with brain morphology (cortical thickness, cortical volume, cortical surface area) as dependent variables. Brain structure is an individual-trait-level neural outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: beta coefficients for cortical thickness associations (Table 2), vertexwise analysis with statistical significance thresholds (Q < 0.05), FDR-corrected findings across prefrontal, temporal, occipital, and parietal lobes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis of social media variable with brain outcomes reported: 'higher average daily social media use was associated with lower cortical thickness' with specific regional findings and statistical p-values throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 2 data only); EC7 requires longitudinal design with social media as DV-only. Not applicable.
notes: Paper pairs social media exposure (IV) with neural outcome (DV). Meets all inclusion criteria for v6 full-text screening.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; N=8,292 participants; baseline ages 9–10 years (2016–2018), Year 2 assessment at mean age 12.0 years (2018–2020); 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort study; longitudinal analysis of Years 2–4 data; generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) with mediation analysis testing smoking expectancies as pathway between social media time and nicotine experimentation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in The American Journal on Addictions (2026); DOI: 10.1111/ajad.70135.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (hours per day at Year 2) is the independent variable; nicotine experimentation (any puff of cigarette, e-cigarette, or smokeless tobacco at Year 4) is the dependent variable. Nicotine experimentation is a substance use outcome (individual-trait-level behavioral variable). Qualifies as social media exposure paired with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: regression coefficient (B=0.14, 95% CI: 0.08–0.20, p<.001) for direct effect; indirect effects mediated by positive smoking expectancies (B=0.009, 95% CI: 0.003–0.014, p=.002); proportion mediated (5.97%, 95% CI: 1.27%–10.67%, p=.013).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with path coefficients and mediation effects; 'Social media time at Year 2 was associated with nicotine experimentation at Year 4' with reported coefficients and p-values for direct and indirect pathways.

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (Years 2→4), but social media is the independent variable (IV), not dependent variable (DV) in the analysis. EC7 only applies when SM is DV-only. Not applicable.
notes: Paper uses prospective mediation design with social media as IV predicting substance use outcome. Meets all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; Year 5 cross-sectional data (2021–2023); N=11,530; ages 13–16 years; from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multivariable linear regression with cluster-robust standard errors estimating association between dating app use and depression symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in BMC Research Notes (2026); DOI: 10.1186/s13104-026-07641-9.

FT_IC2.evidence: Dating app use (lifetime use: yes/no) is a platform-specific social media variable (online dating app qualifies as 'online dating apps / hookup apps' per criteria); paired with depression symptoms (Child Behavior Checklist DSM-Oriented Affective Problems Scale) as the dependent variable. Depression is a mental health/well-being outcome (individual-trait-level).

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative result reported: standardized beta coefficient (ß=0.42; 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.08–0.76; p=0.017) for association between dating app use and depression symptoms in adjusted models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis: 'Dating app use was associated with higher depression symptoms (standardized beta coefficient [ß] 0.42; 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.08–0.76; p=0.017) in adjusted models'; sensitivity analyses confirm association with effect size estimates.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (Year 5 data only). EC7 only applies to longitudinal designs; not applicable to cross-sectional studies.
notes: Paper analyzes dating app use (SM modality) as IV with depression (mental health outcome) as DV. Meets all inclusion criteria for v6.
